request.question: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?

title: The Brothers Cazimero
Mākaha Sons
Theryl DeClouet
Israel Kamakawiwoʻole
Elliot James
Jean Sebastien Gerard
McGruff (rapper)
Truth in Music Advertising
Timothy James Webb
Sons of Hawaii
sentences: The Brothers Cazimero was a Hawaiian musical duo made up of Robert Cazimero on bass and Roland Cazimero on twelve string guitar.
Robert also played piano as a solo musician.
The Cazimeros got their start during the Hawaiian Renaissance with ukulele and slack-key guitarist Peter Moon's band, The Sunday Manoa, on their first recording, "Guava Jam".
Since that time, The Brothers Cazimero have released at least 36 recordings and three DVDs.
For three decades, the group performed at the annual Lei Day Concert.
They made their Carnegie Hall debut in 1989.
The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.
The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.
The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.
After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.
Theryl DeClouet (born September 17, 1951, in Hollygrove, New Orleans, Louisiana), also known as House Man, is a soul/R&B singer, best known as the former lead vocalist for the musical group Galactic.
He appeared on the band's first four studio albums as well as a and a compilation before health concerns forced his departure from the band and its heavy tour schedule.
His latest independent solo release, "The Truth Iz Out", features support from longtime friend Ivan Neville and guitarist June Yamagishi of Papa Grows Funk.
He also appeared on Charlie Hunter Quintet's 2001 album, "Songs from the Analog Playground", Hunter's first to feature vocals.
DeClouet sang on versions of Earth, Wind & Fire's "Mighty Mighty" and the Willie Dixon standard "Spoonful."

Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.
Elliot James Mulhern (born September 29, 1989) known professionally as Elliot James, is a British singer songwriter, producer, and musician also known as the drummer of rock band Hey Monday.
He owns a music publishing and production company Fever Mint.
He left Hey Monday fall of 2009 and toured with various acts including The Scene Aesthetic.
James is the owner of record label, Blossöm Records.
He produced three records on Jallal's album "Off The Radar" with Chad Hugo, Ne-Yo, BJ the Chicago Kid, and Jan Fairchild.
Jean-Sébastien Gerard, born 1970, is a musician also known as Jess from Overlanders, who composed chiptunes for the Atari ST demoscene in the 90s.
Herbert Brown, better known by his stage name McGruff (also known as Herb McGruff) is an American Hip hop recording artist from Harlem, New York City, New York.
Brown started rapping in his early teens.
He embarked on his music career in the hip hop group Bronx Most Wanted, alongside rappers Jay Q and Tee U.B. Brown later became a member of the hip hop collective Children of the Corn.
As a member of Children of the Corn, Brown worked alongside Big L, Cam'ron, Bloodshed and Mase, all of whom would go on to have successful careers in the music industry.

After the group disbanded without releasing any material, Brown made his first appearance on Big L's debut album, "Lifestylez ov da Poor & Dangerous" (1995), on the tracks "8 Iz Enuff" and "Dangerzone".
Eventually, Brown signed a deal with popular hip hop label, Uptown Records and began recording his debut album, "Destined to Be".
In early 1998, the album's lead single "Before We Start" became a minor hit, charting on several "Billboard" charts.
In the Summer of 1998, "Destined to Be" was released but failed to sell many copies, only peaking at 169 on the "Billboard" 200, and Brown was released from his contract.
Brown would make appearances with Heavy D on his album, "Waterbed Hev" and the "Woo" soundtrack.
In 2009, Brown appeared on Mase's mixtape "I Do the Impossible".
In 2010, he was featured heavily on The Diplomats mixtape, "The D.I.P. Agenda."
In 2014, DJ Kay Slay, enlisted Brown, along with Raekwon, Fat Joe, Ghostface Killah, Sheek Louch, N.O.R.E., Lil' Fame, Prodigy and Rell, for a song titled "90s Flow".

The Truth in Music Advertising act or bill, also known as Truth in Music Performance Advertising or simply Truth in Music, is legislation, adopted into state law by most U.S. states, that aims to protect the trademark of musical recording artists.
The legislation provides that the name of a famous musical group cannot be used by a group of performers unless they include at least one member of the original group.
The intent of the legislation is to prevent unfair or deceptive trade practices, and to protect the livelihood of musicians who were in famous musical groups.
Timothy James Webb, as a musician also known as Tim J. Webb (born Warburton, Victoria, 13 February 1967), is an Australian painter and sculptor, who has been living in Munich, Germany, since 2000.
The Sons of Hawaii was a Hawaiian musical group that grew popular to mainstream audiences from the 1960s through the 1990s.

candidate.summarize1.predict:

Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.

You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.

- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.

- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).

Additional constraints (must follow):

If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?
hop1_docs: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.

Alone in IZ World | Alone in IZ World is an album by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole released in 2001, 4 years after his death in 1997.  The album has charted on several of Billboard’s album charts.  These are:

Gosti iz budushchego | Gosti iz budushchego (Russian: Гости из будущего , lit.: "Guests from the Future") was a Russian dance-pop group, which existed since 1996 until 2009.  It was formed by the singer Eva Polna and musician Yuri Usachyov.  The band reached high positions in Russian music charts with several songs and albums.

Kuana Torres Kahele | Kuana Torres Kahele (born September 7, 1978) is a Hawaiian musician, vocalist, songwriter, record producer, dancer, and educator from Hilo, Hawaii.  Known primarily for his original acoustic compositions, Kahele arranges traditional Hawaiian music as well, performing and recording with instruments such as ukulele, guitar, ipu, and bass.  The majority of his lyrics are written and performed in the Hawaiian language.  After learning kane dancing and mele writing in his teens, in 1995 Kahele co-founded Nā Palapalai, a Hawaiian music group that has released seven albums.  Several have charted in the top five on the "Billboard" Top World Albums chart, and the group has won a large number of Nā Hōkū Hanohano Awards, including Group of the Year.
N Dis Life | In Dis Life is the final album of original music by Native Hawaiian singer Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole before his death, released in 1996.  It reached #3 at Top World Music Albums.

Dennis Pavao | Dennis Pavao (July 11, 1951 - January 19, 2002), was one of several Hawaiian musicians who, during the 1970s, led a Hawaiian music renaissance, reviving Hawaiian music, especially "ka leo ki'eki'e," or Hawaiian falsetto singing.  Along with his cousins, Ledward and Nedward Kaʻ apana, Pavao started the group Hui ʻ Ohana.  Hui ʻ Ohana became the premier falsetto group in Hawaiʻ i.  After the breakup of Hui ʻ Ohana, Dennis Pavao moved on to pursue a solo career.

Cold Case Files (album) | Cold Case Files is a compilation album released by rap group, Onyx.  It was released on August 19, 2008 through the Iceman Music Group and contained unreleased tracks from the group's first three albums, Bacdafucup, All We Got Iz Us and Shut 'Em Down.  Deceased member, Big DS and affiliate, X-1 also appear on the album.
response.summary_1:
Israel Kamakawiwoʻole ("Braddah Iz") — primary topic

- Name and identity: "Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole", dates "May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997"; passage: "also called 'Braddah Iz' (Brother Iz)"; described as a "Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist."
- Discography facts: album "Alone in IZ World" (released 2001, posthumous) and "In Dis Life" (final album of original music, released in 1996).
- Missing fact (blocks answer): the specific "Hawaiian musical group" membership is not stated in the provided passages. Bridge entity: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole.
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. Fetch the "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole" Wikipedia page — inspect the lede/infobox and "Early life"/"Career" sections for band/group membership details.
  2. Fetch the "Mākaha Sons of Niʻihau" (variants: "Makaha Sons of Niihau", "Makaha Sons") Wikipedia page — inspect "History" or "Members" sections for Israel Kamakawiwoʻole as a member.
  3. Search keywords: "Israel Kamakawiwo'ole member of band", "Israel Iz member Makaha Sons", and "Israel Kamakawiwo'ole group membership".
response.hop2_query: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole Mākaha Sons of Niʻihau member
hop2_docs: Mākaha Sons | The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.  The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.  The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.  After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.

Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.

Kaleohano | Kaleohano, a name meaning "the voice (of) authority and respect," is the title of a song written by Louis Moon Kauakahi.  The song has been performed by Israel Kamakawiwoʻ ole and by the Mākaha Sons of Niʻ ihau.
Jon de Mello | Jon de Mello born in Honolulu and raised in Waikiki is an American record producer, manager, composer, artist, very famous for producing Israel "IZ" Kamakawiwoʻole.

Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World | "Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World" is a medley of the songs "Over the Rainbow" and "What a Wonderful World" by Israel Kamakawiwoʻole and released on his albums "Ka ʻAnoʻi" and "Facing Future".

Mākaha, Hawaii | Mākaha (Hawaiian for “fierce” or “savage”) is a census-designated place (CDP) in Honolulu County, Hawaiʻ i, United States.  It’s a town located along the Pacific Ocean, west of the Mākaha Valley, and at the foot of Mt. Ka'ala in the Wai’anae Mountain Range.  It is the last of the leeward towns on O'ahu.  North of Mākaha is little development, i.e. no towns, no gas stations, or restaurants.  The population of Mākaha was 8,278 at the 2010 census.  It is located 35 miles northwest of Honolulu, but is a part of Honolulu County.

Mākaha Beach Park | Mākaha Beach Park is a white sand beach in Mākaha, Hawaii, the neighbor of the beaches southeast of Ka‘ena Point such as Yokohama Bay (Keawa‘ula Beach) and Mākua Beach.  It has good surf in winter.
hop2_titles: Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World
response.summary_2: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole was a founding member of the Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau. Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu.
